summary: Policy-backed control for Windows Power Throttling. Good for machines that prioritize background responsiveness, but it can increase power use and reduce battery-life optimizations.

setting.name: Power Throttling
setting.casual_explanation: Windows can slow some background work to save power. This policy decides whether that throttling stays available.
setting.why_people_change_it: People turn it off when they want background tasks to stay more responsive, especially on performance-focused desktops or plugged-in systems.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Turning it off can improve background responsiveness, but it can also reduce one of Windows' power-saving behaviors and may use more battery.
setting.professional_notes: Power.admx maps the Turn off Power Throttling policy under HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Power\PowerThrottling with value name PowerThrottlingOff. The local ADMX file uses enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0. The local ADML help text says enabled turns Power Throttling off, while disabled or not configured leaves the setting under user control.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Windows does not force the policy and users control the setting.

allowed_values.label: Power Throttling not forced off
allowed_values.meaning: The policy is disabled and users control the setting.

allowed_values.label: Power Throttling turned off
allowed_values.meaning: Power Throttling is turned off by policy.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes PowerThrottlingOff = 1 on the documented policy path.

windows_defaults.label: Windows policy default
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows 10 and later client systems exposing the policy
windows_defaults.notes: The local ADML help text says disabled or not configured leaves the setting under user control.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
avoid_for: Performance-focused desktops that want less background throttling
recommended_profiles.good_when: You want Windows to keep its normal power-management behavior.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want to minimize background throttling for responsiveness.
tradeoffs: Background tasks may be throttled more aggressively.

recommended_profiles.label: Performance focused

states.rationale: Turn off Power Throttling by policy.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want less background throttling and steadier task responsiveness.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You prefer Windows power savings over extra responsiveness.
tradeoffs: Background work is less likely to be throttled.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft Power.admx mapping
evidence.summary: The local ADMX file maps the Turn off Power Throttling policy to HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Power\PowerThrottling\PowerThrottlingOff with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft Power.adml help text
evidence.summary: The local ADML help text says enabling the policy turns off Power Throttling, while disabled or not configured leaves the setting under user control.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes PowerThrottlingOff = 1 for the current tweak.

decision.why: The local Microsoft policy files clearly document the path, values, and meaning, and the app writes the documented turn-off value. There is no unresolved edge left for the app path.

validation_proof.notes: Local Microsoft Power.admx defines the exact machine policy path and value name. The companion Power.adml help text explains that enabling the policy turns Power Throttling off, while disabled or not configured leaves it under user control.
